risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "We used permuted block randomization at the level of the patient. Once eligibility was determined, written consent obtained, and the baseline interview completed, the study coordinator opened a sealed envelope containing a computer generated random assignment." Comment: The study explicitly describes a proper random sequence generation method: computer-generated assignment with permuted block randomization. This is sufficient evidence of a truly random allocation sequence.

Quote: "Once eligibility was determined, written consent obtained, and the baseline interview completed, the study coordinator opened a sealed envelope containing a computer generated random assignment." Comment: This indicates use of sealed envelopes, but the report does not explicitly state that the envelopes were sequentially numbered and opaque (i.e., full SNOSE). Therefore, allocation concealment is not adequately described to confirm that the recruiter could not foresee assignment before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Clinical and study staff and study participants were all blinded to assignment until after the baseline interview. The nature of the intervention meant that participants were not blind to the condition after participation in the intervention." Comment: Participants were explicitly unblinded after baseline, and personnel delivering the interventions would also know assignment because the interventions differed clearly (40-minute decision-aid videotape, motivational interviewing session, or attention-control booklet). The main outcomes included subjective and potentially behavior-influenced measures such as patient willingness, knowledge, expectations, and self-reported discussion with the primary care provider, which could plausibly be affected by knowledge of allocation. Therefore performance bias is at high risk.

Quote: "The nature of the intervention meant that participants were not blind to the condition after participation in the intervention." and "Participant interviews were completed at baseline and at 1, 3 & 12 months following the intervention... Baseline and 1 month follow-up interviews included knowledge questions... the willingness rating and the Knee Expectations Scale. Interviews at 3 & 12 months asked about discussion of knee pain with the PCP and included the willingness rating and the Knee Expectations Scale." Comment: The main outcomes included patient-reported willingness, knowledge, expectations, and report of discussion with PCP, so the outcome assessor for these measures was the patient self-report. Because participants were explicitly unblinded after receiving the intervention, and these outcomes are subjective and easily influenced by knowledge of allocation, detection bias is high. Although some 12-month care-use outcomes were "validated by chart review," the study also included unblinded subjective self-reported outcomes; by the worst-outcome rule this domain is High risk overall.

Quote: "639 participants met the study criteria and were randomized to one of the 4 study arms. There were 24 patients who received the baseline assessment but never received the intervention and were not included in the final analysis. There were no losses to follow-up except for one patient in the MI arm and one patient in DA/MI arm. Over the course of the study 93% of the subjects completed at least 2 of the 3 follow-up interviews with no differences among the 4 intervention groups (p=0.62)." Comment: Post-randomization missing outcome data appear minimal for the analysed follow-up outcomes: only 2 losses to follow-up after intervention among 639 randomized participants (~0.3%), with no meaningful imbalance (1 in MI, 1 in DA+MI). The analysis used mixed-effects models for repeated measures, which is an appropriate approach for moderate missingness. Although 24 randomized participants who never received the intervention were excluded from final analysis, the paper reports that subsequent loss to follow-up was essentially complete and balanced; taken together, the reported outcome data suggest low risk of attrition bias for the outcomes of interest.
